channel.title: math.HO updates on arXiv.org
channel.description: math.HO updates on the arXiv.org e-print archive.

item.title: The history of three wrong definitions
item.description: arXiv:2604.24956v1 Announce Type: new Abstract: The topic is the history of the concepts of equivalence relation, Cauchy sequence, and metric space. The thesis is that disused definitions of these notions could profitably be revived.
item.dc:creator: Harold P. Boas

item.title: The Network Structure of Mathlib
item.description: arXiv:2604.24797v1 Announce Type: cross Abstract: The ongoing development of Lean 4's Mathlib has produced a macroscopic structural complexity that interweaves logical, mathematical, and infrastructural dependencies. We present a network analysis of this library, extracting its dependency structure into a multilayer graph of 308,129 declarations, 8.4 million edges, and 7,563 modules. By introducing graph decompositions that isolate explicit edges from those synthesized by the compiler or driven by proofs, we quantify the structural properties of formalized mathematics. Our analysis reveals three findings. First, taxonomies designed by humans diverge from logical structures, exhibiting a 50.9% coupling across namespaces. Second, developers utilize a median of 1.6% of the imported scope. Third, formalization compresses semantic hierarchies, with network centrality capturing language infrastructure rather than mathematical relevance.
item.dc:creator: Xinze Li, Nanyun Peng, Simone Severini, Patrick Shafto

item.title: The Inverse Cube Force Law
item.description: arXiv:2604.24799v1 Announce Type: cross Abstract: Newton's Principia is famous for its investigations of the inverse square force law for gravity. But in this book Newton also did something that remained little-known until fairly recently. He figured out what kind of central force exerted upon a particle can rescale its angular velocity by a constant factor without affecting its radial motion. This turns out to be a force obeying an inverse cube law! Here we discuss this and some other interesting features of the inverse cube force law.
item.dc:creator: John C. Baez

item.title: Computational Platonism
item.description: arXiv:2506.19787v3 Announce Type: replace Abstract: We offer a view of mathematics as an experimental science where axioms play the role of foundational theories like general relativity and quantum mechanics in physics. Under this view, axioms are provisional and inferred from experience with the experiental substrate of mathematics which we locate within computation rather than encoding intuitive and absolute truths. This offers a reframing of Godel's theorem, placing its impact sharply upon the incompleteness rather than the potentially contradictory nature of any computational set of axioms. The essay originated in an attempt to make precise the nature of mathematics in order to estimate how AI might impact it. This exploration is continued in the paired essay "The mechanical creation of mathematical concepts."
item.dc:creator: Asvin G

item.title: Hexagonal Tiling of the Plane
item.description: arXiv:2508.01404v2 Announce Type: replace-cross Abstract: Since the thesis of K. Reinhardt in 1918, it is well known that there are exactly three types of convex hexagons that can tile the plane. However, the proof of the fact is far from being complete. We prove this fact, under an assumption weaker than the convexity.
item.dc:creator: Ze Zhu, Erxiao Wang, Min Yan
